text:
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Okonkwo, 63-year-old female with known stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (right kidney primary, pT3b pN2 M1), status post open right radical cytoreductive nephrectomy. Patient currently on first-line ipilimumab plus nivolumab maintenance (nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks). Concurrent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained separately has demonstrated marked enlargement of pulmonary metastases and interval increase in pelvic nodal disease, consistent with progressive systemic disease. MRI brain obtained to evaluate for intracranial metastatic involvement prior to initiation of second-line systemic therapy. History of intermediate IMDC risk, sarcomatoid differentiation, and BAP1 loss.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3-Tesla magnet without and with intravenous gadolinium-based contrast (gadobutrol, 0.1 mmol/kg). Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1, axial T1 pre- and post-contrast, axial T2, axial T2 FLAIR, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), and axial gradient recalled echo (GRE). Dedicated thin-section (1 mm isotropic) post-contrast 3D T1 MPRAGE sequence obtained for metastasis protocol. Images compared to MRI brain from approximately 9 months prior (obtained at the time of initial staging workup, prior to nephrectomy).

FINDINGS:

Metastatic Disease Survey:
No enhancing intracranial lesions are identified on the post-contrast T1 and MPRAGE sequences. Careful review of all brain compartments -- including the cerebral hemispheres, cerebellar hemispheres, cerebellum vermis, brainstem (midbrain, pons, and medulla), cranial nerve cisterns, leptomeningeal surfaces, and calvarium -- demonstrates no focal areas of abnormal gadolinium enhancement suspicious for parenchymal, leptomeningeal, or dural metastatic deposits. No discrete parenchymal nodules or masses are seen on any sequence. No evidence of hemorrhagic metastases on susceptibility-weighted imaging or GRE. No midline shift, subfalcine herniation, or uncal herniation.

Cerebral Hemispheres:
The cerebral cortex is intact and without focal signal abnormality. No gyral swelling or cortical diffusion restriction. Normal gray-white matter differentiation. Mild, scattered T2/FLAIR hyperintensities are present in the periventricular and subcortical white matter of both cerebral hemispheres, predominantly involving the frontal and parietal lobes. These nonenhancing foci are unchanged in distribution and extent compared to the prior examination and are most consistent with chronic microvascular ischemic changes in the setting of the patient's known hypertension and type 2 diabetes. No new white matter lesions are identified.

Ventricular System and Sulci:
The lateral ventricles, third ventricle, and fourth ventricle are normal in size and configuration. The cerebral sulci and Sylvian fissures are mildly prominent but symmetric, appropriate for the patient's age. No transependymal edema or hydrocephalus.

Basal Ganglia, Thalami, and Internal Capsules:
No focal signal abnormality. No lacunar infarcts. Normal T2 signal and morphology bilaterally.

Brainstem and Cerebellum:
The midbrain, pons, and medulla are unremarkable in signal and morphology. The cerebellar hemispheres and vermis demonstrate no focal lesions, no enhancement, and no diffusion restriction. The fourth ventricle is midline and unobstructed.

Sellar and Parasellar Region:
The pituitary gland is normal in size and signal characteristics. No sellar or suprasellar mass. The infundibulum is midline. Given the patient's prior immunotherapy with ipilimumab, the pituitary is specifically interrogated for signs of hypophysitis: no gland enlargement, no stalk thickening, and no abnormal enhancement are identified.

Calvarium and Skull Base:
The calvarium demonstrates normal marrow signal on T1-weighted sequences, without evidence of focal marrow replacement or destructive osseous lesions to suggest cranial vault metastatic disease. The skull base and clivus are unremarkable. No epidural or subdural collections.

Vascular Structures:
The major intracranial arterial flow voids appear maintained on T2 sequences. The dural venous sinuses appear patent on susceptibility-weighted imaging. No arteriovenous malformation or vascular anomaly.

Other Findings:
No acute infarct is identified on diffusion-weighted imaging or ADC mapping. No intracranial hemorrhage. Minimal mucosal thickening within the bilateral maxillary sinuses, a nonspecific incidental finding. The orbits and visualized craniofacial soft tissues are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. No intracranial metastatic disease. No enhancing parenchymal, leptomeningeal, or dural lesions are identified on this dedicated metastasis-protocol MRI with gadolinium. The brain is clear of involvement by the patient's known metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma.

2. Unchanged mild scattered periventricular and subcortical white matter T2/FLAIR signal changes compared to the prior examination from approximately 9 months ago, consistent with established chronic small vessel ischemic disease in the context of longstanding hypertension and diabetes mellitus. No progression of these changes over the interval.

3. No imaging features of ipilimumab-related hypophysitis. Pituitary gland morphology and enhancement are within normal limits.

4. No acute intracranial abnormality.

Correlate clinically with systemic disease status. No intracranial findings are identified to explain the patient's reported dyspnea or cough; the pulmonary and nodal progression documented on concurrent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis is the more likely etiology of her symptoms.
